title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)
The patient.
Mila Makovec.
Symptom onset age 3, diagnosis at 6, first dose late January 2018, paper published October 2019, died February 2021 at age 10.
The disease.
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis, CLN7 subtype.
Caused by loss of MFSD8, a lysosomal membrane transporter.
Part of the Batten disease family of lysosomal storage disorders.
Fatal pediatric neurodegeneration with vision loss, language regression, seizures, ataxia.
The mutations.
Compound heterozygous.
Allele 1: c.1102G>C — known pathogenic point mutation, detected by gene panel.

Allele 2: ~2 kb SVA retrotransposon insertion in intron 6 — missed by panel, found by whole-genome sequencing + manual inspection of chimeric reads (poly-T tail, AGAGGG hexamer repeats, 14 bp target site duplication = SVA signature).
The molecular mechanism.
SVA introduced a cryptic splice acceptor (i6.
SA) ~119 bp upstream of real exon 7 acceptor → mis-splicing → premature stop codon → truncated non-functional protein.
The drug.
Milasen (research name TY777).
22-nucleotide 2'-MOE phosphorothioate antisense oligonucleotide.
Splice-modulating mechanism — masks the cryptic acceptor and nearby exonic splicing enhancers so the spliceosome uses the real exon 7 acceptor instead.
Cell validation.
In patient fibroblasts, tripled correct splicing (8% → 34%); half-maximal effective concentration in nanomolar range; rescued lysosomal vacuoles, autofluorescence, autophagic flux.
Preclinical.
Abbreviated rat intrathecal study at 0.06, 0.25, 1.0 mg. 0.06 mg uneventful; higher doses caused transient hindlimb weakness resolving by ~24 h.
Platform safety inherited from nusinersen.
Dosing.
Intrathecal injection.
Loading: escalating 3.5 → 7 → 14 → 21 → 28 → 35 → 42 mg, plus two more at 42 mg, approximately every 2 weeks.
Maintenance: 42 mg every ~3 months.
42 mg = molar equivalent of 3× nusinersen, chosen because brain distribution is ~1/3 of spinal cord.
Total drug substance manufactured: ~18 g.
Outcomes (first year).
No serious adverse events.
Seizures: 63% reduction in daily count (31.5 → 11.7/day), 52% reduction in duration (108s → 52s), 85% reduction in % time in seizure (3.9% → 0.6%).
No change in antiepileptic regimen.
Vineland-II, GMFM-88, Bayley, sensory thresholds: stabilized rather than continuing to decline.
MRI: continued brain volume loss — neurodegeneration slowed clinically but not reversed.
CSF pharmacokinetics: dose-proportional rise, plateau at 18–27 ng/ml.
Timeline (key dates).
Nov 2016 — clinical diagnosis.
Jan 2017 — referral to Yu's lab.
Feb 2017 — SVA mutation identified.
May 2017 — patient fibroblasts established + splice defect identified.
Aug 2017 — ASO hit.
Oct 2017 — ASO function validated.
Dec 2017 — rat toxicology study.
Jan 2018 — FDA Expanded Access permission.
Late Jan 2018 — first patient dose.
Oct 2019 — NEJM paper published.
Feb 2021 — Mila died at age 10.
Precedent drug.
Nusinersen (Spinraza), approved Dec 2016 for spinal muscular atrophy.
Same 2'-MOE phosphorothioate chemistry, same intrathecal delivery, same splice-modulating mechanism.
The platform validation that made milasen's abbreviated preclinical program acceptable.
Regulatory pathway.
FDA Expanded Access (compassionate use) IND.
Authorized for use in Mila specifically, not approved as a drug.
Boston Children's IRB review with extensive family consent process.
Institutional aftermath.
Boston Children's Oversight Committee on Personalized Experimental Therapeutics.
FDA draft guidance on individualized ASO development (2020–2021).
n-Lorem Foundation founded by Stanley Crooke to provide custom ASOs for free to qualifying rare-disease patients.
Five dominoes knocked over.
A brand-new drug can be designed and dosed in one patient in <1 year.
ASO chemistry is portable across diseases when the splice defect is amenable.
FDA template established for individualized IND submissions.
Rare-disease N-of-1 movement triggered.
Honest limits established: slows progression, does not reverse damage already done.
Master analogies.
ASO = a sticky note placed over a word in a sentence so the reader's eye skips over it.
Splice-switching ASO = sticky note that hides one bad word so the splicing machinery picks the right boundary.
SVA = a parasitic Word macro that has been copying itself for millions of years.
Cryptic splice acceptor = a fake doorway intercepting traffic meant for the real exit.
Panel testing vs whole-genome sequencing = checking a list of usual suspects vs reading the whole book.
Compound heterozygous = two broken copies, broken in different ways, one from each parent.
Lysosome = the cell's recycling center.
N-of-1 trial = a clinical study with sample size 1.
Intrathecal delivery = spinal injection bathing the brain and spinal cord directly.
Diagnostic odyssey = 5–10 years and dozens of specialists before a diagnosis.
